name.en-US: Commentary on the De Interpretatione (Peri Hermeneias)

description.en-US: Aquinas's commentary on Aristotle's *De Interpretatione* (*Peri Hermeneias*) — on names, verbs, propositions, contradiction, and modality. Unfinished — Aquinas covered the first book.
description.la: Expositio *Peri Hermeneias* Aristotelis — de nominibus, verbis, propositionibus, contradictione, modalitate. Opus inchoatum — Librum I tractavit.

sources.description: English translation by Jean T. Oesterle (Marquette, 1962), revised by the Aquinas Institute. Mirrored from aquinas.cc.
Latin text from the Leonine / Marietti editions (public domain), mirrored from aquinas.cc.

title.en-US: Book 1 — Dedication
Prologue — Dedication
Book 2
Lecture 1 — Introduction
Lecture 2 — The signification of vocal sound
Lecture 3 — The diverse signification of vocal sound
Lecture 4 — The name
Lecture 5 — On the nature of the verb and its conformity with the name
Lecture 6 — On speech, the formal principle of the enunciation
Lecture 7 — The definition of enunciation
Lecture 8 — The division of enunciation into simple and composite, affirmative and negative
Lecture 9 — The opposition of affirmation and negation absolutely
Lecture 10 — The proposition’s division on the part of the subject; affirmation and negation in universal and indefinite propositions
Lecture 11 — The opposition of universal and particular enunciations; the relation of affirmation and negation to truth and falsity
Lecture 12 — Only one negation opposed to one affirmation
Lecture 13 — Truth and falsity in opposed singular propositions about the future in contingent matter
Lecture 14 — Contingency in things and the roots of contingency in relation to singular propositions about the future in contingent matter
Lecture 15 — It is concluded that propositions are true as they correspond to the way in which things are in reality
Book 3
Lecture 1 — The distinction and order of simple enunciations, in which the finite or the infinite name is posited only on the part of the subject
Lecture 2 — The number and relationship of simple enunciations, in which the verb “is” is predicated as a third element and the subject is the finite name not universally taken
